title: Inspired
author: Marty Cagan
summary: A practical reminder that product work is not feature delivery, but solving meaningful customer problems with strong teams.
takeaways: Discovery quality usually decides whether delivery matters.
Good product teams align business, design, and engineering early.

title: Atomic Habits
author: James Clear
summary: Small behavior shifts compound when the environment and the feedback loop are designed on purpose.
takeaways: Environment design beats motivation when consistency matters.
Tracking can be a product feature, but identity change is the real engine.

title: The Mom Test
author: Rob Fitzpatrick
summary: A concise playbook for asking better product questions and avoiding polite but useless customer feedback.
takeaways: Ask about real behavior, not future intentions.
Bad customer conversations create false confidence very quickly.

title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
author: Martin Kleppmann
summary: A deep read on reliability, data systems, and the infrastructure tradeoffs that appear once products grow beyond simple CRUD.
takeaways: System design quality comes from understanding tradeoffs, not memorizing components.
Data flow decisions should reflect user experience priorities.

title: Deep Work
author: Cal Newport
summary: A useful lens for protecting attention and designing work rhythms that support meaningful output.
takeaways: Depth needs system support, not just discipline.
High-quality work often comes from deliberate constraints.

title: Build
author: Tony Fadell
summary: A candid operator's view on building products, teams, and judgement over time.
takeaways: Taste is developed through repetition and reflection.
Product decisions get sharper when they stay close to user pain.
